coredata.dc:description: The abnormal aggregation of β-amyloid protein (Aβ) is a major contributor to Alzheimer's disease (AD). Cu2+ homeostasis imbalance can lead to the aggregation of Aβ, resulting in cytotoxic oligomers and fibrous aggregates, causing neuroinflammation and nerve cell damage, ultimately leading to AD. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (CQD), and designed a macrophage membrane (RAW-M) encapsulated CQD nanosystem for the first time. The abundant nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CQD effectively capture excess Cu2+ and inhibit rapid Aβ aggregation. Additionally, the good photothermal properties of CQD dissolve the formed fiber precipitates under near-infrared light (NIR). In vitro and in vivo studies showed that the nanosystem significantly improved BBB permeability under laser irradiation, enhancing its ability to cross the BBB and overcome traditional anti-AD drug limitations. In vivo investigations conducted on APP/PS1 mice indicate that the nanosystem strongly reduced Aβ deposition, mitigated neuroinflammation, and ameliorates deficits in learning and memory. Overall, our nanocarrier approach adjusts metal ion homeostasis, inhibits abnormal Aβ aggregation, and uses excellent photothermal properties to depolymerize mature Aβ fibrils to protect cells from Aβ neurotoxicity, providing an effective strategy for Aβ-targeted treatment of AD.

(A) The variation of fluorescence intensity of CQD-RAW at 470 nm with the increase of Cu2+ concentration from 10 μM to 100 μM. (B) The concentration of copper ions (black) and the calculated chelation rate (red) in the remaining solution of CQD and CQD-RAW, respectively. (mean ± SD, n = 3) (C) The fluorescence intensity of ThT treated with Aβ monomers alone. Aβ monomers + Cu2+ and Aβ monomers + Cu2++CQD and CQD + RAW. Temperature changes of CQD (D) and CQD-RAW (E) with different concentrations under NIR (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 10 min). (F) The fluorescence intensity of ThT treated with Aβ fibrils alone, Aβ fibrils with NIR, Aβ fibrils in the presence of CQD + RAW with/without NIR (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 10 min). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A)TEM images of Aβ oligomers alone, Aβ oligomers + Cu2+ and Aβ oligomers + Cu2++CQD and CQD-RAW incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, bar = 200 nm. (B)TEM images of Aβ fibrils alone, Aβ fibrils + CQD and CQD-RAW (20 μg/mL) with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm,2 W/cm2, 10 min), bar = 200 nm. CD spectra of Aβ fibrils alone and co-incubated with CQD (C) and CQD-RAW (D) with or without light (808 nm,2 W/cm2, 10 min).

(A) Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of CQD-RAW uptake by SH-SY5Y cells (bar = 50 µm). (B)The relative survival rate of SH-SY5Y cells incubated with CQD and CQD-RAW for 24 h (mean ± SD, n = 3); (C) The alleviation effect of different concentrations of CQD and CQD-RAW on the cytotoxicity of Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates (mean ± SD, n = 3). (D) The alleviation effect of CQD-RAW on the cytotoxicity of Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates (mean ± SD, n = 3). Aβ-Cu2+ complex was prepared by Aβ (10 μM) and Cu2+ (20 μM) in a molar ratio of 1:1.

BALB/c mice were injected with PBS, CQD-RAW through the tail vein, and the fluorescence map (A) and fluorescence value (B) of the brain were obtained after 8 h with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2,5 min) (mean ± SD, n = 3). (C) Record of temperature change within 4 h after injection (mean ± SD, n = 3).

(A)Movement track of mice in each group in open field test. The total distance of movement (B), the relative rest time (C), the number of standing (D), the number of turning back (E), the time of staying in the central area (F) and the percentage of activity in the central area (G) of the experimental mice in the open field test. (mean ± SD, n = 5).

(A) Typical swimming trajectory of mice. Comparison of the escape delay time of the learning path between different groups of mice (B) The percentage of time spent swimming in the escape platform quadrant (C) The number of times the target platform quadrant (D). WT mice were the control group. (mean ± SD, n = 5).

(A) Immunohistochemical staining of hippocampus after various treatments. The white circle indicates that Aβ deposition (green) is obvious. (B) In situ apoptosis of hippocampal neurons (red). The white square indicates obvious neuronal apoptosis. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The preparation flow chart of CQD-RAW and the schematic diagram of the CQD-RAW nanosystem for the combined treatment of AD. The nanosystem has high BBB permeability with the help of NIR. It can capture excessive Cu2+ to inhibit Aβ aggregation and eliminate mature Aβ fibrils through excellent photothermal properties.

head.ce:title: Macrophage membrane-encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanosystem for targeted treatment of Alzheimer's disease: Regulating metal ion homeostasis and photothermal removal of β-amyloid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abnormal aggregation of β-amyloid protein (Aβ) is a major contributor to Alzheimer's disease (AD). Cu2+ homeostasis imbalance can lead to the aggregation of Aβ, resulting in cytotoxic oligomers and fibrous aggregates, causing neuroinflammation and nerve cell damage, ultimately leading to AD. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (CQD), and designed a macrophage membrane (RAW-M) encapsulated CQD nanosystem for the first time. The abundant nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CQD effectively capture excess Cu2+ and inhibit rapid Aβ aggregation. Additionally, the good photothermal properties of CQD dissolve the formed fiber precipitates under near-infrared light (NIR). In vitro and in vivo studies showed that the nanosystem significantly improved BBB permeability under laser irradiation, enhancing its ability to cross the BBB and overcome traditional anti-AD drug limitations. In vivo investigations conducted on APP/PS1 mice indicate that the nanosystem strongly reduced Aβ deposition, mitigated neuroinflammation, and ameliorates deficits in learning and memory. Overall, our nanocarrier approach adjusts metal ion homeostasis, inhibits abnormal Aβ aggregation, and uses excellent photothermal properties to depolymerize mature Aβ fibrils to protect cells from Aβ neurotoxicity, providing an effective strategy for Aβ-targeted treatment of AD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a frequently occurring degenerative illness that affects the nervous system[1]. Memory loss and cognitive impairment are hallmark symptoms of the disease[2]. The disease is widely regarded as a significant global public health crisis, given its devastating impact on individuals and society as a whole[2,3]. AD is primarily characterized by the formation of extracellular senile plaques (SP) and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles (NFT)[4], which result in synaptic dysfunction and neuronal death[5], likely caused by the neurotoxic effects of the Aβ peptide[6,7], as suggested by the widely accepted Aβ toxicity hypothesis[7-9]. At the same time, it also triggers downstream Tau protein hyperphosphorylation, activates glial cells to induce neuroinflammation, and increases oxidative stress injury[4,9,10]. This hypothesis has been continuously deepened and verified in recent years. Therefore, inhibiting the aggregation process of Aβ is one of the important ways to prevent and treat AD[11,12]. Based on the evidence at hand, it appears that the disturbance of metal ion balance, especially an increase in Cu2+ levels, can initiate the creation of Aβ aggregates[13-15]. The presence of copper ions bound to histidine residues and Aβ hastens the aggregation and deposition of Aβ, leading to an accumulation of senile plaques and neurofibrillary tangles[16,17]. Proinflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-1 (IL-1) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) are expressed around Aβ plaques produced during neurodegeneration in AD, interferon and macrophage migration inhibitory factor is also gradually increasing, leading to chronic neuroinflammation[18,19]. Considering the etiology of AD, inhibiting the aggregation of Aβ induced by Cu2+ and destroying Aβ fibrils could be a potential treatment strategy to manage the progression of the disease[20]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a novel non-invasive treatment method that is gaining popularity that involves the use of light absorbers to convert laser energy into heat energy, which produces a local super-energy body temperature. Nanoparticles can effectively convert absorbed near-infrared (NIR) light energy into heat energy when exposed to it, resulting in a sharp rise in local temperature that can decompose Aβ fibrils. Therefore, combining PTT with a metal chelating agent has become a popular approach in AD treatment design to enhance the overall therapeutic effect[21-24].

Nowadays, a variety of nanoagents or nanomedicines in the field of neuroscience can block Aβ aggregation[9]. Small-molecule drugs or peptides have demonstrated efficacy in eliminating Aβ aggregation, but their clinical application is hindered by their limited ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB). Although disrupting the BBB could facilitate the penetration of nanodrugs into brain tissue, this poses a significant risk to their clinical utility[25-30]. Functionalized nanomaterials have the advantages of low toxicity, good biocompatibility and degradability, which will not lead to inflammation and interfere with the normal function of BBB. They can also use the existing physiological transport mechanism to interact with cells in BBB and promote the transport of nanoparticles from blood to brain[31-34]. Therefore, it has become the most promising candidate drug to overcome the limitation of BBB and achieve therapeutic effect. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are a novel form of quasi-spherical quantum carbon-based nanomaterial with a dimension of less than 10 nm made up mostly of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. There are several functional groups and surface groups/polymer chains on its surface that increase water solubility, such as carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and amines[35]. There are many functional groups on its surface and a large number of surface groups that promote water solubility. Due to its unique physical, optical, and chemical properties, CQD are considered a promising therapeutic drug [36]. Because the surface of CQD is easy to be modified, and has good biocompatibility and stability, CQD are commonly used in various applications such as bioimaging[37], drug delivery[38], photoacoustic/photothermal therapy[39]. Recent studies have confirmed that the surface of CQD has strong ligands to adsorb Cu2+ through chelation[14,36,40]. In view of the aforementioned challenges, the construction of a new nano-delivery system with a high blood–brain barrier based on CQD is the most ideal AD processing method. This study utilized CQD as a substrate material due to the abundant nitrogen-containing groups on their surface that can chelate Cu2+ and improve Cu2+-mediated Aβ aggregation[14]. Additionally, CQD possess excellent photothermal properties and can act as PTT nanoagents to enhance and increase the permeability of the BBB, leading to local hyperthermia that can dissolve Aβ amyloid deposits[41].

Targeted therapy is an important application of precision medicine in the field of neuroscience. Alzheimer's disease is an inflammatory disease as the presence of Aβ plaques can activate inflammatory cells and alter levels of related anti-inflammatory mediators[42]. In order to enhance the targeting performance of CQD, we encapsulated a layer of macrophage membrane outside the CQD to construct a macrophage membrane-encapsulated CQD (CQD-RAW), giving it the characteristic of immune evasion and inflammatory targeting. This results in improved efficacy of the nanocomposite at the targeted site, and the NIR irradiation enables the opening of BBB, further enhances the material's brain accumulation. The in vivo efficacy of this treatment was evaluated in APP/PS1 mice, leading to a reduction in brain Aβ deposition, neuroinflammation, and improvements in learning and memory deficits (Scheme 1 ).

Citric acid and urea were procured from McLean, FITC was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich in the USA. DIR dye was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Shanghai. Shanghai Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. supplied penicillin–streptomycin, 4 % paraformaldehyde, and β-amyloid peptide (1–42). DMEM and FBS were purchased from HyClone Thermo Scientific. MTT was sourced from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., the microplate reader was acquired from Bio Rad, and the atomic absorption spectrophotometer was obtained from Shanghai Element Analysis Instrument Co., Ltd. Shimadzu provided the fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC). All other reagents were of analytical purity.

The present study proposes a solvothermal method for synthesizing quantum dots using citric acid and urea as starting materials. The first step involved preparing a solution comprising 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea dissolved in 10 mL of dimethyl methylamide (DMF). The mixture was then subjected to a high-temperature reaction in a reactor at 160 °C for 6 h. The solution produced after the reaction was transferred to a centrifuge tube and subjected to centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 15 min to remove any large particles. The resulting solution was then dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis membrane with a mesh size of 10,000 to remove any ions or small particles that hadn't reacted yet. The last step involved combining the solution with ethanol in a 1:5 ratio and then subjecting the mixture to centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting precipitate was collected and washed with deionized water to obtain the final solution of CQD. The solution was freeze-dried to obtain powder, and the concentration of CQD was determined.

RAW 264.7 cells were grown under standard cell culture conditions until the cell growth area reached 80% to 90%. The cells were then trypsinized and the resulting cell suspension was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 min. The cell pellet obtained at the bottom of the centrifuge tube was collected. The cells were then dispersed in an appropriate amount of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and sonicated for 20 min under ice bath conditions (200 W). The obtained solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min and the clear supernatant was collected. The supernatant was sonicated again for 10 min in an ice water bath to obtain the macrophage membrane (RAW-M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Macrophage membrane modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the macrophage membrane-modified CQD (CQD-RAW), a dispersion of CQD was first obtained by ultrasonication in a water bath for 10 min at 200 W. The macrophage membrane (RAW-M) was then mixed with CQD in a certain proportion and 3 mL of PBS with pH 7.4 was added. The resulting mixture was oscillated at 37 °C for 12 h. Next, 1 mL of PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) was added to the mixture and it was subjected to water bath ultrasound for 5 min at 200 W to obtain a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL of CQD-RAW.

The diameter and Zeta potential of the CQD, RAW-M, and CQD-RAW samples were determined using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS). Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was utilized to observe the surface morphology of the samples, while the element distribution on the sample surface was characterized by TEM-Mapping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of copper ion chelating properties
ce:section.ce:para: CQD and CQD-RAW dispersions were added to PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4), and calculated amounts of Cu2+ ions (10 μM to 100 μM) were added. The fluorescence intensity of CQD-RAW was measured at room temperature. Then the ability of CQD-RAW to capture Cu2+ was measured by atomic absorption spectrometry. The Cu2+ solution (5 μg/ml) (C1) and the CQD-RAW solution were mixed at a mass ratio of 1:1. After incubation for 30 min, the mixed solution was filtered through an ultrafiltration membrane. The lower solution was an unchelated Cu2+ solution. Finally, the Cu2+ concentration of the lower solution (C 2) was determined by atomic absorption spectrophotometer, and the Cu2+ chelation rate of the material was calculated. Chelation rate=(C1-C 2)/C1 × 100%.

A near-infrared laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2) was used to irradiate CQD and CQD-RAW solutions at concentrations of 0.2 mg/mL, 0.1 mg/mL and 0.05 mg/mL for 10 min. The temperature of the material was observed synchronously using a near-infrared thermal imager and recorded every 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of inhibiting Aβ peptide aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: The Aβ peptide was re-suspended in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 5 mM) before use, and Aβ monomers were obtained by sonication for 10 min in a water bath. Dilute DMSO monomer solution (5 mM) with deionized water to a concentration of 100 µM and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to obtain Aβ oligomers. For the experiment of inhibiting Cu2+ to accelerate Aβ aggregation, Aβ monomer was incubated with Cu2+, CQD and CQD-RAW in a 37 °C water bath for 2 days. After 30 min incubation with 10 mM ThT solution, ThT fluorescence assay was performed with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). For the depolymerization experiment of Aβ fibrils, Aβ self-aggregation into Aβ fibrils was achieved by mixing Aβ monomers in the bulk solution into PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and incubating for 7 days in a 37 °C water bath. Aβ fibers were mixed with CQD and CQD-RAW, irradiated with NIR laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2) for 10 min, and then incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, and the fluorescence intensity was then scanned at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The sample was obtained after the same treatment and its morphology was observed by transmission electron microscopy.

The culture of SH-SY5Y cells involved the use of DMEM Medium supplemented with 15% FBS and 1% penicillin and streptomycin, and the cells were maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2 to facilitate their growth. RAW-M was labeled with FITC, and CQD-RAW was synthesized and added to SH-SY5Y cells in a glass bottom culture dish for 2 h. The cells were then washed with PBS, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and stained with DAPI before imaging with a laser confocal microscope. Cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells was determined by MTT assay after 24 h of co-culture with CQD and CQD-RAW at varying concentrations (2.5 ∼ 50 μg/mL). For the experiment of nanomaterials inhibiting the cytotoxicity of Aβ-Cu2+ complex, Aβ monomer was co-cultured with Cu2+ and SH-SY5Y cells under different conditions (with or without CQD and CQD-RAW) for 24 h. After incubation with MTT reagent for 4 h, the cell viability was detected by MTT method at 570 nm. In the depolymerization experiment of Aβ fibrils by NIR, Aβ monomer was incubated in advance for 7 days to form fibrils, and then co-incubated with SH-SY5Y cells. After treatment with 10 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, and 50 μg/mL CQD and CQD-RAW for 24 h, the cells were irradiated with NIR (808 nm and 1.5 W/cm2) for 5 min, and the cell viability was detected by MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on in vitro transport of blood–brain barrier
ce:section.ce:para: This experiment simulated an in vitro blood–brain barrier model using bEnd.3 cells and SH-SY5Y cells co-cultured in a transwell plate. The upper chamber was inoculated with bEnd.3 cells to form a tight monolayer, and the lower chamber was filled with SH-SY5Y cells. FITC-labeled CQD-RAW was added to the upper chamber and allowed to incubate with bEnd.3 cells for 24 h. Then, the cells were irradiated with NIR (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2, 5 min) in the light group, while the non-irradiation group was not treated. A fluorescence microscope was employed to visualize the green fluorescence signal in the SH-SY5Y cells. This experiment aimed to evaluate whether CQD-RAW can cross the BBB in vitro and enter the SH-SY5Y cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo near infrared thermal imaging investigation
ce:section.ce:para: Our study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Jiangsu University (UJS-IACUC-2022052701). The experiment involved injecting fluorescent dye DIR-labeled CQD-RAW into female C57/BL6 mice (n = 3) via the tail vein at a dosage of 5 mg/kg. The mice were divided into two groups: the light group was exposed to NIR (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2, 5 min) at 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, and 4 h after injection, while the control group received only a PBS injection. After 8 h, all mice were sacrificed, and their brains were dissected. The fluorescence intensity of the brain tissue was imaged and analyzed using a small animal fluorescence imager. The purpose of this experiment was investigate the ability of DIR-labeled CQD-RAW to cross the blood–brain barrier and accumulate in brain tissue, as well as the effect of NIR on this process.

The study involved two types of mice - WT mice (C57/BL6) and transgenic mice (APP/PS1) that are known to develop Alzheimer's disease-like pathology. All the mice were kept under standard laboratory conditions for six months before the start of the experiment. The mice were then divided into five groups, each containing five mice. The groups were: WT group (WT mice treated with PBS),APP/PS1 group (transgenic mice treated with PBS),CQD-RAW treatment group (APP/PS1 mice treated with CQD-RAW (5 mg/kg)),PBS-treated light group (APP/PS1 mice treated with PBS and exposed to NIR),CQD-RAW-treated light group (APP/PS1 mice treated with CQD-RAW (5 mg/kg) and exposed to NIR).The mice in the CQD-RAW treatment group and the CQD-RAW-treated light group received tail vein injections of CQD-RAW (5 mg/kg) once every 2 days for 4 weeks. The mice in the PBS-treated light group and CQD-RAW-treated light group were exposed to NIR at 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, and 4 h time intervals after injection. The open field experiment is a commonly used behavioral test to assess locomotor activity and exploratory behavior in rodents. The open field experiment was conducted during the third week of administration. The mice under experimentation were placed in the detection room an hour before the formal test. The entire experiment was carried out in a quiet environment, and the open field infrared tracking system was utilized to automatically record the autonomous activity of the mice within a five-minute interval. Movement distance, number of returns, residence time in the central area, percentage of distance, and number of uprights were analyzed. The analysis of these parameters can help us gain insights into the level of activity and exploratory behavior of the mice. By evaluating the movement distance, number of returns, and residence time in the central area, researchers can determine whether the mice are more active and exploring more of the open field or if they are staying in one place. The percentage of distance and number of uprights can also indicate the level of anxiety or exploration exhibited by the mice.

Following the onset of Alzheimer's disease, it is common to experience symptoms such as cognitive dysfunction and decline in memory. The Morris water maze (MWM) test is a common tool used to assess learning and memory ability, and it was utilized to evaluate the therapeutic effect of composite materials in vivo. After the last dose, mice underwent training and testing for the MWM. Prior to the water maze experiment, the experimental mice were pre-trained for 7 days. During the experiment, the mice were placed in the quadrant opposite to the quadrant where the platform was located. If the mice could reach the platform and stay for 3 s, it would automatically stop. If they were unable to find the platform, they were manually helped to reach it within 10 s. Normal mice gradually learn to locate the platform during the training period. After training, the platform was removed, and the mice were placed in the opposite quadrant of the platform, and their swimming path and escape latency were recorded.

After behavioral testing, the brains of all mice were collected and fixed after death. Brain samples were embedded, sectioned, and stained. Thioflavin S staining was used to detect Aβ plaques in the hippocampus of mice, TUNEL staining was used to detect the level of positive nuclear apoptosis to observe the brain injury of mice, and H&E staining was used to detect the morphological changes of brain tissue cells. The levels of proinflammatory cytokines were quantified using the Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) technique.

The objective of this investigation was to create a nanocomposite CQD-RAW with the potential for multi-target AD therapy. The synthesis pathway of CQD-RAW and its utilization in AD treatment are presented in Scheme 1. Initially, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized by one-pot method. Next, the cell membrane was extracted from macrophage RAW 264.7. The CQD-RAW composite was produced via an ultrasound-assisted and mixed incubation approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, Particle size and Zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of CQD (Fig. 1 A) revealed its size to be approximately 5 nm, while after wrapping with macrophage membrane, a vesicle-like material with a size of about 50 nm was formed (Fig. 1B), which was advantageous for crossing the BBB. Particle size and Zeta potential of CQD with or without modification were determined by DLS. The particle size of CQD and RAW-M was found to be 5.15 ± 0.21 nm and 46.81 ± 2.01 nm, respectively (Fig. 1C). After modification with macrophage membrane, the diameter of CQD-RAW significantly increased to 51.16 ± 2.13 nm, consistent with the TEM results. Thus, successful modification of CQD by macrophage membrane could be inferred. The Zeta potential of CQD was −30.79 ± 1.31 mV, and the Zeta potential of RAW-M was −40.49 ± 2.81 mV (Fig. 1D). The Zeta potential of CQD-RAW was close to that of macrophage membrane (−36.76 ± 2.1 mV), indicating successful encapsulation of CQD on the macrophage membrane. Larger Zeta potential implies greater stability, and the absolute value of potential increased after modification, indicating improved stability.

The TEM-mapping image of CQD-RAW (Fig. S1A) clearly demonstrated that RAW-M encapsulated a significant amount of CQD, forming a vesicular structure that proved the successful construction of the nanocomposite. Interestingly, from the elemental distribution of CQD-RAW in (Fig. S1B-D), it was apparent that the N (C) and C (D) of CQD are distributed synchronously with the P (B) signal present only in the macrophage membrane, further confirming that the macrophage membrane effectively encapsulated the quantum dots.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was utilized to identify the surface functional groups present in CQD. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig.S2) exhibited a strong peak at 3413 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of the NH portion. Furthermore, a strong peak at 1623 cm−1 was observed, indicating the presence of CN stretching vibration. The absorption at 1353 cm−1 provided further evidence of the presence of CN bonds. These results confirmed the existence of nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as –NH, CN, and CN, on the surface of CQD. The abundance of hydrophilic groups on CQD confers good water solubility and stability in aqueous solutions. The plentiful nitrogen-containing groups also enable CQD to exhibit high binding capacity for metal ions, such as Cu2+, which makes it possible to inhibit Aβ aggregation through chelation of Cu2+.

The composition of CQD and the content of N element were investigated by elemental analysis. According to the result, the proportions of N, C and H elements in CQD were 36.09%, 12.17% and 3.71%, respectively. XPS was used to further explore the surface structure of CQDs. The atomic percentages for C, N, and O at the surfaces of the CQDs were 77.96%, 18.21%, and 3.83%, respectively (Fig. S3). The results proved the nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQD. The XPS spectrum of CQD (Fig. S3a) has three peaks at 285.1, 400.1 and 531.1 eV, belonging to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S3b) has three peaks of 284.5, 285.5 and 287.7 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of CC, CN and CO, respectively [36]. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. S3c), the two peaks with binding energies of 399.5 and 401.5 eV would respectively correspond to NC and NH. The O1s spectrum of CQD (Fig. S3d) shows two peaks, CO at 531.1 eV and CO/OH at 532.8 eV [37].
In addition, CQD has a wide fluorescence excitation range, with a maximum excitation wavelength of 340 nm. At this excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelength of CQD was 470 nm (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of copper ion chelating properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering the significant impact of Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation on AD pathogenesis[43], we investigated the therapeutic potential of CQD-RAW nanomaterials to chelate Cu2+ and inhibit the abnormal aggregation of Aβ. Fluorescence is one of the excellent properties of CQD (Fig.S4), and then we use this performance to evaluate its ability to chelate Cu2+. The results showed a positive correlation between the increase of Cu2+ concentration from 10 μM to 100 μM and the significant decrease in fluorescence intensity at 470 nm (Fig. 2
A). This suggests that CQD is effective in capturing copper ions and quenching their fluorescence.

Furthermore, the residual Cu2+ concentration in the remaining solution was determined by an atomic absorption spectrophotometer to calculate the chelating ability of CQD and CQD-RAW for Cu2+. The introduction of CQD and CQD-RAW caused a rapid and significant decrease in the remaining Cu2+ concentration within a short period of time (Fig. 2B). The concentration of copper ions in the remaining solution was 1.84 ± 0.16 μg/ml and 1.122 ± 0.12 μg/ml, respectively. The calculated copper ion chelation rate of CQD-RAW was 77.56 ± 2.13%, slightly higher than that of CQD (63.2 ± 1.57%). This may be due to the macrophage membrane wrapped outside CQD, which also adsorbed some Cu2+. These results showed that Cu2+ can be effectively captured and absorbed by the material.

Abnormal metal ion homeostasis in the brain is the main factor leading to the accelerated aggregation of Aβ in AD[44]. To investigate the ability of CQD-RAW to inhibit Cu2+-accelerated Aβ aggregation by Thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence, we used ThT, which specifically binds to the β-sheet of Aβ fibrils. The change in fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm and the emission wavelength of 485 nm reflects the change in amyloid fibrils content. The fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the amount of β-fold. Higher fluorescence intensity indicates more β-folding in Aβ aggregates. The Aβ monomer was mixed with Cu2+ solution, and CQD and CQD-RAW were added at the same time. The fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer after incubation with ThT solution (10 mM) for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of Aβ in the presence of Cu2+ was greater than that of the control group where Aβ was incubated alone. (Fig. 2C), indicating that under the action of Cu2+, Aβ monomer accelerated the transition to β-amyloid fibrils. After the addition of CQD-RAW, fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, illustrating that CQD-RAW chelated a lot of Cu2+ and effectively restrained the Cu2+-mediated Aβ fibril aggregation. Photothermal ablation of amyloid aggregates is a method of AD treatment. Local hyperthermia can decompose amyloid by interfering with the growth of amyloid deposition[22]. The photothermal conversion capability of the material was evaluated by measuring the heating effect. CQD-RAW at a concentration of 0.2 mg/ml could be heated up to 52.8 °C in 10 min (Fig. 2E), slightly lower than CQD (Fig. 2D). According to the calculation formula of photothermal conversion efficiency[45], based on Fig.S5 (A) and (B), the photothermal conversion efficiency of CQD-RAW is 39.65%, indicating that the material still has good photothermal conversion characteristics after being modified by the macrophage membrane.

In the photothermal elimination experiment of Aβ fibrils, CQD and CQD-RAW were mixed with Aβ fibrils and divided into an illumination group and a non-illumination group. The illumination group was exposed to near-infrared laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 10 min) and then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Fluorescence intensity was measured using the ThT method. The addition of CQD-RAW significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of ThT (Fig. 2F), indicating the successful decomposition of some amyloid deposits. It is worth noting that the fluorescence of the solution decreased remarkably after NIR irradiation, indicating a decrease in the amount of Aβ fibrils present, which is quite fascinating. This confirmed that CQD-RAW could successfully decompose most mature Aβ fibrils through photothermal effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the structure and morphology of Aβ
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy was used to visualize the microstructure of the samples during the inhibition of Aβ monomers and photothermal elimination of Aβ fibrils. In the material inhibiting Aβ aggregation experiment, the control group showed typical mature fibrils formed from Aβ aggregates. The addition of Cu2+ further accelerated the formation of Aβ aggregates, resulting in a markedly different morphology compared to Aβ fibrils without Cu2+, showing both agglomerated and amorphous Aβ aggregates (Fig. 3A). Morphological observations also indicated that CQD-RAW was effective in inhibiting the abnormal aggregation of Aβ peptides in vitro.

After incubation with Aβ monomer for 7 days, typical 1–2 μm Aβ fibrils were formed, showing a large number of aggregated fibrils (Fig. 3B). Long dendritic Aβ fibrils disappeared after NIR irradiation of CQD and CQD-RAW, providing significant morphological evidence for the depolymerization of Aβ fibrils. This indicates that CQD-RAW effectively depolymerized a large amount of Aβ fibrils under the photothermal effect driven by NIR laser.

Circular dichroism (CD) is a commonly used method for determining protein secondary structure. The CD spectrum of optically active molecules, such as proteins and DNA, in the solution under test is used to analyze their secondary structure[46]. In the CD spectrum of Aβ aggregates, a negative peak of 218–225 nm was detected (Fig. 3C), indicating that the Aβ aggregates had a random coil structure. Changes in the peak position indicate changes in the secondary structure of the Aβ protein. Interestingly, when the Aβ solution was incubated with CQD and CQD-RAW solutions, the negative absorption peak gradually shifted to the left, possibly due to the reduction of random coils and the transition to β-sheets. After near-infrared illumination, the negative absorption peak rapidly weakened, suggesting that the Aβ aggregates were no longer in a β-sheet structure and could not form a typical amyloid fibril structure.

We conducted further investigations on the cellular uptake of nanocomposites. Firstly, we labeled CQD-RAW with FITC to synthesize fluorescent CQD-RAW, and co-cultured it with SH-SY5Y cells for 4 h. The SH-SY5Y cells' ability to uptake CQD-RAW was demonstrated in the laser confocal image (Fig. 4 A). and were mainly located in the cytoplasm. The easy intake of materials is beneficial for improving the effectiveness of AD treatment. Moreover, the targeting effect of macrophage membrane can not only target the carrier modified by macrophage membrane to the inflammatory site, but also endow the immune escape ability to reach the lesion site more efficiently.

Ensuring safety is crucial for the development of drug carriers in treating diseases. To assess cytotoxicity, we conducted an MTT assay and found that cells incubated with varying concentrations of CQD and CQD-RAW for 24 h had a relative cell viability above 90% (Fig. 4B). This indicates that both unmodified and modified materials are not significantly cytotoxic. Our findings suggest that the CQD-RAW nanosystem is a safe treatment option for Alzheimer's disease.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alleviation of the cytotoxicity of Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: Given the vital role of Cu2+ in accelerating Aβ aggregation in the pathogenesis of AD, we conducted this study to investigate the impact of CQD and CQD-RAW on the cytotoxicity of Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregates. To achieve this, we mixed equal molar ratios of Aβ monomer solution and Cu2+ solution and added varying concentrations of CQD and CQD-RAW to co-culture with SH-SY5Y cells for 24 h. We then determined cell viability via MTT assay. Our findings demonstrated that Cu2+ induced Aβ aggregates caused significant damage to cells, leading to a cell survival rate of only 38.61 ± 2.12% (Fig. 4C). However, the introduction of CQD and CQD-RAW resulted in much less decrease in cell viability compared to the Cu2+-induced cytotoxicity of Aβ aggregates. The survival rate of CQD-RAW cells was notably increased to 87.25 ± 3.24% when the highest concentration used was 50 μg/ml. These results confirm that the nanomaterials chelated a certain amount of Cu2+ and effectively inhibited the accelerated aggregation of Aβ caused by Cu2+, thereby resulting in the highest recovery of cell viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal elimination of Aβ fibrils and reduce cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: To verify whether the photothermal properties of the material can break down β-amyloid fibrils and reduce their toxicity, we co-cultured CQD and CQD-RAW with Aβ fibrils and SH-SY5Y cells for 24 h, and divided them into two groups: light and non-light. The cells in the light group were irradiated with NIR (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 5 min), and cell survival was observed after 24 h. The cell viability of the Aβ group was the lowest at 31.57 ± 1.34% (Fig. 4D). However, with the increase in concentration of CQD and CQD-RAW, cell viability gradually increased. When the concentration of CQD-RAW was 50 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was 87.25 ± 3.24%, indicating that CQD-RAW effectively digested most of the Aβ fibrils under the action of NIR. The results confirmed that CQD-RAW synergistically degraded most of the Aβ toxic aggregates under the combined action of chelating Cu2+ and photolysis, effectively alleviating its toxicity.

The treatment of nervous system diseases is hindered by the presence of the blood–brain barrier, which limits the passage of therapeutic agents. However, several studies have indicated that the near-infrared photothermal effect can enhance the permeability of the BBB. To overcome this obstacle, we investigated the photothermal effect of CQD-RAW to enhance the light transmittance of the barrier. To establish an in vitro BBB model, culturing of bEnd.3 cells in the upper chamber and SH-SH5Y cells in the lower chamber was conducted using the BBB model. The FITC-labeled CQD-RAW treatment group showed a green fluorescence signal (Fig.S6), indicating that CQD-RAW had the ability to cross the BBB. Remarkably, after NIR photothermal treatment at 808 nm (2 W/cm2 for 5 min), the intensity of the green fluorescence signal significantly increased, suggesting that the near-infrared photothermal effect of CQD-RAW significantly improves its efficiency of crossing the BBB, thus enhancing the effect of AD treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo near infrared thermal imaging investigation
ce:section.ce:para: The brain fluorescence imaging results further confirmed that NIR can enhance the permeability of materials through the BBB. The CQD-RAW + NIR group showed a greater fluorescence signal as compared to the CQD-RAW group, with a value of 13.87 ± 3.21 × 109 (Fig. 5 A-B). The fluorescence intensity in the brain after illumination was about 58.9% higher than that in the non-illuminated group (Fig. 5B), indicating successful opening of the BBB under the action of near-infrared laser irradiation, leading to improved transmittance of nanomaterials.

In addition, the changes in brain temperature before and after illumination were measured by near-infrared imager. At a predetermined time point, the brain temperature difference in the CQD-RAW + NIR group gradually increased compared to the PBS control group, with a temperature difference of 11.14 ± 0.87 °C after 4 h of material injection (Fig. 5C). This may be due to the opening of BBB after NIR, which increased the accumulation of CQD-RAW in the brain and enhanced the material's photothermal conversion ability.

In current behavioral studies, open field experiments are commonly used to assess the spontaneous activity of mice. The path diagrams of the mice intuitively showed the total distance traveled by each group (Fig. 6 A).

In the experimental group, compared to the PBS group, mice in the CQD-RAW + NIR group exhibited the longest distance traveled and the highest frequency, which was similar to the wild group. The total distance traveled by mice in the CQD-RAW + NIR group (65.5 ± 2.74) m was greater than that in the PBS + NIR group (43.8 ± 2.64) m, and was similar to that in the wild group (63.5 ± 2.85) m (Fig. 6B). Correspondingly, the relative rest time of the CQD-RAW + NIR group (Fig. 6C) was the shortest in the experimental group, similar to the wild-type group, reaching 20.15 ± 2.74%. This indicated that the spontaneous activity of the CQD-RAW + NIR group was the highest, which was consistent with the total distance traveled results. Similar to the movement ability, the CQD-RAW + NIR group also showed the highest number of standing times (Fig. 6D), turning back (Fig. 6E), time spent in the central area (Fig. 6F), and distance traveled (Fig. 6G).

The results of the open field experiment showed that tension and anxiety behavior of mice decreased after treatment, resulting in increased activity and improved exploration ability. Compared to other groups, the treatment effect of the CQD-RAW + NIR group was the most significant.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a well-known cause of memory loss and cognitive dysfunction. In our study, we used the Morris water maze to investigate the effects of composite materials on learning and memory in mice. The results revealed that AD mice treated with PBS and PBS + NIR displayed random and disordered swimming patterns (Fig. 7 A), indicating impaired learning and memory functions. Conversely, AD mice treated with CQD-RAW demonstrated increased frequency of passing through the target quadrant, which contained the platform. More importantly, there was no significant difference between the CQD-RAW + NIR group and the WT group. This implies that after the combined treatment of chelating Cu2+ and NIR, we were able to reduce the memory impairment of mice and improve their spatial learning ability. APP/PS1 mice in the CQD-RAW + NIR group displayed a shorter escape latency than the other groups, particularly on the fifth day of training. This performance was comparable to that of the WT group (Fig. 7B). Furthermore, the combined treatment group's mice demonstrated a preference for the target quadrant by spending more time there and crossing the target platform more often (Fig. 7C and D). Based on the findings, it can be inferred that the memory impairment and cognitive dysfunction observed in the CQD-RAW + NIR group were significantly ameliorated in comparison to the other groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amyloid plaque staining and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
ce:section.ce:para: After 4 weeks of treatment, the brain tissues of mice in each group were taken for thioflavin S staining, TUNEL and H&E staining to observe the morphology of cells and neurons, and the level of Aβ in the hippocampus and peripheral areas. Compared with the PBS and PBS + NIR control groups, a large number of different Aβ Following a four-week treatment period, thioflavin S staining, TUNEL and H&E staining were used to observe brain tissue morphology and Aβ levels in mice. Results showed that Aβ depositions were extensively observed around the neurons in the DG region of the PBS and PBS + NIR control groups, but less in the CQD-RAW treatment group, and almost disappeared in the CQD-RAW + NIR treatment group (Fig. 8 A). The ability of CQD-RAW nanomaterials to chelate Cu2+ around neurons prevented the accumulation and deposition of Aβ, and also successfully digested Aβ fibrils under NIR. Moreover, the level of neuronal apoptosis was lower in the CQD-RAW + NIR treatment group than in the PBS and PBS + NIR control groups (Fig. 8B), indicating that CQD-RAW reduced Aβ-induced neuronal toxicity and protected neurons in vivo. Based on the findings, it can be inferred that CQD-RAW was successful in accelerating the removal of Aβ aggregates and significantly reducing the neuronal toxicity caused by Aβ aggregates with the help of NIR.

To ensure in vivo therapeutic safety, the potential toxicity of CQD-RAW was assessed by H&E staining of brain tissue. The results in Fig.S7 showed that compared to the WT and PBS groups, the CQD-RAW and CQD-RAW + NIR groups showed no significant changes in the hippocampus and peripheral regions, and no obvious histopathological abnormalities were observed, indicating good biocompatibility of the nanomaterials and safe in vivo application of CQD-RAW. ELISA was used to detect inflammation-related factors in the hippocampus. Results showed that compared to the PBS control group, the expression levels of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α in the brain of AD mice in the CQD + NIR group were decreased. (Fig.S8) These findings suggest that the combination of photothermal ablation of amyloid plaques and regulation of metal ion homeostasis can effectively reduce the inflammatory response in the brain of AD mice, reduce cell damage, and delay the pathological process of AD.

In this study, a nanocomposite material with metal ion chelation and photothermal conversion was successfully developed and utilized for the multi-target treatment of Alzheimer's disease with promising outcomes. CQD demonstrated excellent dispersibility in water and high biocompatibility. Compared with the existing literature[47,48], The macrophage membrane encapsulation strategy reduced the recognition of CQD by macrophages in vivo, evading immune system clearance. Additionally, CQD's photothermal properties enabled it to efficiently cross the BBB and target the lesion site faster, aided by NIR and the macrophage membrane's inflammatory targeting characteristics. Moreover, CQD's surface N-containing functional groups chelate Cu2+ to inhibit Aβ aggregation, while its superior photothermal properties effectively clarify Aβ1-42 deposition under NIR irradiation. In vitro cell experiments confirmed high biosafety and the ability of CQD-RAW to inhibit Aβ1-42 neurotoxicity in a concentration-dependent manner. Finally, animal experiments and immunohistochemical studies demonstrated the improvement of cognitive function and learning and memory impairment in AD mice, resulting from inhibiting Aβ monomer aggregation, decomposing and removing Aβ fibrils, and alleviating peripheral neuroinflammation. In order to fully understand the therapeutic effect of CQD-RAW in vivo, further studies on the distribution and toxic effects of CQD-RAW in vivo are needed. In summary, the multi-target synergistic nanosystem designed in this study has great potential for Alzheimer's disease treatment, providing a new avenue for future nanotechnology applications in treating Alzheimer's disease.

Jichun Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Meng Xie: Design, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
